issue.title: Chore: restore Superset README context and remove remediation app copy
issue.body:
## Problem

The repository README currently appears to describe the agentic remediation application rather than the Superset fork.

This is confusing for contributors because the README should help them understand the target repository, local setup expectations, and how this fork relates to Apache Superset.

## Why it matters

A correct README improves contributor experience, reduces confusion during review, and keeps the forked Superset repository clearly separated from the remediation automation repository.

## Scope

Review the current README and make a focused documentation correction so the repository is clearly presented as the Superset fork used for remediation issues.

Keep the change small. Do not rewrite unrelated documentation or modify application code.

## Acceptance criteria

- Update the README title and opening section so it clearly refers to the Superset fork.
- Remove or replace references that describe the agentic remediation app as if it is the main project.
- Add a short note that this fork is used as the target repository for remediation workflow testing, if appropriate.
- Do not change Superset application code.
- Open a pull request with a clear summary of the documentation correction.
- Comment on this issue with the PR link and validation result.

## Validation

This is documentation-only. Automated validation is not required, but the PR should state that the README was reviewed for clarity.

issue.title: Add baseline security headers to the Docker NGINX reverse proxy
issue.body:
# Add baseline security headers to the Docker NGINX reverse proxy

## Type
Security / Hardening

## Priority
Medium

## Summary
The Docker NGINX reverse proxy configuration does not define common defensive HTTP response headers. Adding baseline headers would improve the default security posture for local demos, development, and proof of concept environments.

## Context
The Docker NGINX configuration proxies Superset traffic, but does not appear to set common security headers such as:

- `X-Content-Type-Options`
- `Referrer-Policy`
- `X-Frame-Options`
- `Permissions-Policy`

## Why this matters
Although this configuration is primarily used for local development, Docker based setups are often reused for demos, testing, and customer proof of concepts.

Adding conservative security headers reduces accidental exposure and improves security hygiene without materially changing application behavior.

## Suggested remediation
Add baseline security headers to the NGINX server configuration.

Example:

```nginx
add_header X-Content-Type-Options "nosniff" always;
add_header Referrer-Policy "strict-origin-when-cross-origin" always;
add_header X-Frame-Options "SAMEORIGIN" always;
add_header Permissions-Policy "geolocation=(), microphone=(), camera=()" always;
```

Do not introduce a strict Content Security Policy unless it is fully tested against the Superset frontend.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] NGINX responses include `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`
- [ ] NGINX responses include a conservative `Referrer-Policy`
- [ ] NGINX responses include `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN`
- [ ] NGINX responses include a conservative `Permissions-Policy`
- [ ] Superset UI still loads correctly through the Docker NGINX proxy
- [ ] No restrictive CSP is added unless separately tested

## Suggested validation
Run Superset through Docker and check response headers:

```bash
curl -I http://localhost
```

Expected result: the configured security headers are present in the response.

issue.title: Make streaming CSV export chunk size configurable
issue.body:
# Make streaming CSV export chunk size configurable

## Type
Quality / Maintainability

## Priority
Medium

## Verified in repo
The streaming CSV export chunk size is hardcoded to `1024` in:

- `superset/charts/data/api.py`
- `superset/sqllab/api.py`

Both locations create streaming CSV export commands using a fixed chunk size.

## Summary
Streaming CSV exports currently use a hardcoded chunk size. This should be configurable so operators can tune export behavior for different deployment sizes and workloads.

## Why this matters
A fixed chunk size may not be suitable for all deployments. Smaller environments may prefer smaller chunks to reduce memory pressure, while larger environments may prefer larger chunks to improve throughput.

Making this configurable improves operational flexibility while preserving the existing default behavior.

## Suggested remediation
Add a Superset config value such as:

```python
CSV_EXPORT_CHUNK_SIZE = 1024
```

Then replace hardcoded usage like:

```python
chunk_size = 1024
```

with config-driven logic, for example:

```python
chunk_size = app.config.get("CSV_EXPORT_CHUNK_SIZE", 1024)
```

The implementation should safely fall back to `1024` if the configured value is missing, invalid, or non-positive.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `superset/charts/data/api.py` uses a config-driven CSV export chunk size
- [ ] `superset/sqllab/api.py` uses a config-driven CSV export chunk size
- [ ] Default behavior remains `1024`
- [ ] Invalid or non-positive config values fall back safely to `1024`
- [ ] Tests cover default, custom, and invalid chunk size behavior

## Suggested validation
Run the relevant backend tests for chart export and SQL Lab export.

Suggested test areas:

```bash
pytest tests/unit_tests/commands/chart/streaming_export_command_test.py
pytest tests/unit_tests/commands/sql_lab/streaming_export_command_test.py
pytest tests/unit_tests/sqllab/api_test.py
```

issue.title: Bind Docker-exposed Superset services to localhost by default
issue.body:
# Bind Docker-exposed Superset services to localhost by default

## Type
Security / Network Exposure

## Priority
Medium

## Verified in repo
In `docker-compose.yml`, some services are already bound to localhost:

```yaml
127.0.0.1:${REDIS_PORT:-6379}:6379
127.0.0.1:${DATABASE_PORT:-5432}:5432
```

However, these services expose ports without an explicit localhost binding:

```yaml
${NGINX_PORT:-80}:80
${SUPERSET_PORT:-8088}:8088
```

issue.title: Remove unused POC-only dashboard ExampleComponent
issue.body:
# Remove unused POC-only dashboard ExampleComponent

## Type
Quality / Code Cleanup

## Priority
Low

## Verified in repo
The repo contains a POC-only dashboard component under:

- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/dashboardComponents/ExampleComponent/ExampleComponent.tsx`
- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/dashboardComponents/ExampleComponent/index.ts`

Both files include comments saying the component is POC-only and can be removed after PR approval.

The component is not registered in:

- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/presets/dashboardComponents.ts`

It is only referenced in commented example code in:

- `superset-frontend/src/setup/setupDashboardComponents.ts`

## Summary
A POC-only dashboard component remains in the frontend codebase even though it is not registered or used. It should be removed to reduce dead code and avoid confusion for contributors.

## Why this matters
Leaving unused POC code in the main codebase creates noise and makes it harder for contributors to understand which dashboard component patterns are production-ready.

This is a small cleanup that improves maintainability without changing runtime behavior.

## Suggested remediation
Remove the unused `ExampleComponent` files and update the setup comments so they do not point to a deleted example component.

Possible changes:
- Delete `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/dashboardComponents/ExampleComponent/ExampleComponent.tsx`
- Delete `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/dashboardComponents/ExampleComponent/index.ts`
- Update or simplify the commented example in `superset-frontend/src/setup/setupDashboardComponents.ts`

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] POC-only `ExampleComponent` files are removed
- [ ] No active imports reference `ExampleComponent`
- [ ] Setup comments no longer point to deleted files
- [ ] Frontend typecheck or lint passes

## Suggested validation
Run:

```bash
cd superset-frontend
npm run type
npm run lint
```

issue.title: Treat Docker default SUPERSET_SECRET_KEY as an insecure default
issue.body:
# Treat Docker default SUPERSET_SECRET_KEY as an insecure default

## Type
Security / Configuration Hardening

## Priority
High

## Verified in repo
`docker/.env` contains:

```env
SUPERSET_SECRET_KEY=TEST_NON_DEV_SECRET
```

Superset startup validation currently checks for the built-in default secret key constant, but this Docker-specific value is different. That means the Docker default can bypass the existing insecure default secret check.

Relevant files:
- `docker/.env`
- `superset/config.py`
- `superset/constants.py`
- `superset/initialization/__init__.py`

## Summary
The Docker default `SUPERSET_SECRET_KEY` should be treated as an insecure default and should fail fast or warn loudly outside debug and test contexts.

## Why this matters
Superset uses `SECRET_KEY` for security-sensitive application behavior. A predictable default secret weakens any environment that accidentally reuses the Docker `.env` value beyond local development.

The current value is clearly named as non-production, but relying on a comment is not enough. The application should actively detect it.

## Suggested remediation
Add a constant for the Docker default secret, for example:

```python
TEST_NON_DEV_SECRET_KEY = "TEST_NON_DEV_SECRET"
```

Then update `check_secret_key()` in `superset/initialization/__init__.py` so it treats both values as insecure defaults:

```python
CHANGE_ME_TO_A_COMPLEX_RANDOM_SECRET
TEST_NON_DEV_SECRET
```

Behavior should remain permissive for debug and testing, but production-like startup should fail or emit a clear warning.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `TEST_NON_DEV_SECRET` is treated as an insecure default secret
- [ ] Debug and test startup behavior remains developer-friendly
- [ ] Non-debug, non-test startup refuses to start or clearly fails when this secret is used
- [ ] Warning message explains how to generate a secure secret
- [ ] Unit tests cover the Docker default secret path

## Suggested validation
Add or update tests under:

```bash
tests/unit_tests/initialization/
```

Suggested test command:

```bash
pytest tests/unit_tests/initialization/
```

issue.title: Move semantic layer connection sorting and pagination closer to the query layer
issue.body:
# Move semantic layer connection sorting and pagination closer to the query layer

## Type
Quality / Performance

## Priority
Medium

## Verified in repo
`superset/semantic_layers/api.py` contains a TODO:

```python
# TODO: move sort + pagination to SQL before GA.
```

The current implementation fetches database and semantic layer connection records into Python lists, combines them, and then relies on Python-side sorting and pagination.

## Summary
Semantic layer connection listing should avoid loading more records than necessary into memory. Sorting and pagination should be pushed closer to the SQL query layer where practical.

## Why this matters
Python-side sorting and pagination can become inefficient as the number of databases and semantic layer objects grows. For larger deployments, metadata APIs should remain predictable and avoid unnecessary memory usage.

This is especially relevant because the existing TODO explicitly calls out that this should be addressed before GA.

## Suggested remediation
Refactor the semantic layer connection listing flow so that filtering, sorting, and pagination are applied at the query level where possible.

The implementation should preserve:
- Existing authorization behavior
- Existing filtering behavior
- Existing response shape
- Existing sort order
- Existing pagination semantics

If a full SQL-level refactor is too large, implement a narrower first PR that:
- Adds targeted tests around the current sorting and pagination behavior
- Documents the limitation clearly
- Reduces unnecessary loading where safe

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Existing API response shape is unchanged
- [ ] Authorization filtering is preserved
- [ ] Sorting behavior is covered by tests
- [ ] Pagination behavior is covered by tests
- [ ] The TODO is either resolved or replaced with a more precise follow-up
- [ ] No regression for mixed database and semantic layer connection results

## Suggested validation
Add or update tests covering:
- Sorting by name
- Sorting by changed date
- Pagination with limit and offset
- Restricted user visibility
- Mixed database and semantic layer connection results

issue.title: Migrate TimeTable frontend tests away from global Jest APIs
issue.body:
# Migrate TimeTable frontend tests away from global Jest APIs

## Type
Quality / Test Maintainability

## Priority
Low

## Verified in repo
Several TimeTable test files contain repeated eslint suppressions for global Jest APIs.

Examples include:

- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/TimeTable/utils/valueCalculations/valueCalculations.test.ts`
- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/TimeTable/utils/sparklineHelpers/sparklineHelpers.test.ts`
- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/TimeTable/utils/sparklineDataUtils/sparklineDataUtils.test.ts`
- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/TimeTable/utils/sortUtils/sortUtils.test.ts`
- `superset-frontend/src/visualizations/TimeTable/utils/colorUtils/colorUtils.test.ts`

The files include comments such as:

```ts
// eslint-disable-next-line no-restricted-globals -- TODO: Migrate from describe blocks
```

## Summary
The TimeTable tests should import Jest test helpers explicitly instead of relying on restricted globals and repeated eslint suppression comments.

## Why this matters
Repeated lint suppressions make the test suite noisier and reduce confidence in lint rules. Migrating these tests to explicit imports improves consistency and removes technical debt without changing product behavior.

## Suggested remediation
Update the affected test files to explicitly import Jest helpers.

Example:

```ts
import { describe, expect, it } from '@jest/globals';
```

Then remove the related `no-restricted-globals` eslint suppression comments.

issue.title: Fail websocket startup when JWT secret uses placeholder value
issue.body:
# Fail websocket startup when JWT secret uses placeholder value

## Type
Security / Authentication Hardening

## Priority
High

## Verified in repo
The websocket example config contains a placeholder JWT secret:

```json
"jwtSecret": "CHANGE-ME-IN-PRODUCTION-GOTTA-BE-LONG-AND-SECRET"
```

`superset-websocket/src/index.ts` checks for short secrets and warns when the secret starts with `CHANGE-ME`, but it does not fail startup for the placeholder value.

Relevant files:
- `superset-websocket/config.example.json`
- `superset-websocket/src/index.ts`
- `superset-websocket/spec/config.test.ts`
- `superset-websocket/spec/index.test.ts`

## Summary
The websocket service should fail startup when a placeholder JWT secret is used in a production-like environment.

## Why this matters
The websocket JWT secret protects async event channels. A predictable placeholder secret weakens authentication if copied into a real deployment.

A warning is easy to miss. Production-like startup should fail fast.

## Suggested remediation
Update startup validation so that when the server is started and `NODE_ENV` is not `development` or `test`, placeholder secrets are rejected.

Example policy:
- Fail if `jwtSecret` starts with `CHANGE-ME`
- Fail if `jwtSecret` is shorter than 32 characters
- Keep tests and local development behavior explicit

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Placeholder websocket JWT secrets fail startup in production-like mode
- [ ] Short secrets continue to fail startup
- [ ] Test and local development behavior remains manageable
- [ ] Unit tests cover placeholder, short, and valid secrets
- [ ] Error message explains how to set a secure secret

## Suggested validation
Run websocket tests:

```bash
cd superset-websocket
npm test
```

issue.title: Replace unsafe yaml.load usage in example config loading
issue.body:
# Replace unsafe yaml.load usage in example config loading

## Type
Security / Safe Parsing

## Priority
Medium

## Verified in repo
`superset/examples/utils.py` uses `yaml.load()` with `yaml.Loader`:

```python
metadata = yaml.load(contents.get(METADATA_FILE_NAME, "{}"), Loader=yaml.Loader)
```

The line is currently suppressed with:

```python
# noqa: S506
```

## Summary
Example config loading should use `yaml.safe_load()` instead of `yaml.load()` where arbitrary Python object construction is not required.

## Why this matters
`yaml.load()` can be unsafe when parsing untrusted YAML. Even if example loading is expected to operate on local project files, using `safe_load()` is the safer default and removes the need for a security lint suppression.

## Suggested remediation
Replace:

```python
yaml.load(..., Loader=yaml.Loader)
```

with:

```python
yaml.safe_load(...) or {}
```

Ensure the behavior remains correct when metadata is missing or empty.

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `superset/examples/utils.py` no longer uses `yaml.load()` for metadata loading
- [ ] `# noqa: S506` is removed
- [ ] Missing metadata still defaults to an empty dictionary
- [ ] Example import behavior remains unchanged
- [ ] Tests cover metadata with and without a `type` field

## Suggested validation
Run:

```bash
pytest tests/unit_tests/examples/
pytest tests/integration_tests/importexport/
```

issue.title: Stop printing the Docker admin password during initialisation
issue.body:
# Stop printing the Docker admin password during initialisation

## Type
Security / Sensitive Information Exposure

## Priority
Medium

## Verified in repo
`docker/docker-init.sh` logs the admin username and password during initialisation:

```bash
echo_step "2" "Starting" "Setting up admin user ( admin / $ADMIN_PASSWORD )"
```

## Summary
The Docker initialisation script should not print the admin password to stdout.

## Why this matters
Docker logs are often shared during troubleshooting, demos, CI runs, and customer proof of concepts. Printing credentials into logs increases the chance that a password is accidentally exposed.

## Suggested remediation
Update the log message so it does not include the password.

Example:

```bash
echo_step "2" "Starting" "Setting up admin user ( admin / [hidden] )"
```

or:

```bash
echo_step "2" "Starting" "Setting up admin user"
```

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `docker/docker-init.sh` no longer prints `ADMIN_PASSWORD`
- [ ] Admin user creation behavior remains unchanged
- [ ] Cypress-specific behavior remains unchanged
- [ ] The init logs still clearly show progress

## Suggested validation
Run the Docker init flow and confirm logs do not include the admin password.

```bash
docker compose up superset-init
```

issue.body:
# Fail websocket startup when JWT secret uses placeholder value

## Type
Security / Authentication Hardening

## Priority
High

## Verified in repo
The websocket example config contains a placeholder JWT secret:

```json
"jwtSecret": "CHANGE-ME-IN-PRODUCTION-GOTTA-BE-LONG-AND-SECRET"
```

`superset-websocket/src/index.ts` checks for short secrets and warns when the secret starts with `CHANGE-ME`, but it does not fail startup for the placeholder value.

Relevant files:
- `superset-websocket/config.example.json`
- `superset-websocket/src/index.ts`
- `superset-websocket/spec/config.test.ts`
- `superset-websocket/spec/index.test.ts`

## Summary
The websocket service should fail startup when a placeholder JWT secret is used in a production-like environment.

## Why this matters
The websocket JWT secret protects async event channels. A predictable placeholder secret weakens authentication if copied into a real deployment.

A warning is easy to miss. Production-like startup should fail fast.

## Suggested remediation
Update startup validation so that when the server is started and `NODE_ENV` is not `development` or `test`, placeholder secrets are rejected.

Example policy:
- Fail if `jwtSecret` starts with `CHANGE-ME`
- Fail if `jwtSecret` is shorter than 32 characters
- Keep tests and local development behavior explicit

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Placeholder websocket JWT secrets fail startup in production-like mode
- [ ] Short secrets continue to fail startup
- [ ] Test and local development behavior remains manageable
- [ ] Unit tests cover placeholder, short, and valid secrets

- If dependency or security files change, run package manager, lockfile, dependency, test, or build validation where practical.
- If documentation-only changes are made, state that automated validation is not applicable.
- If validation cannot be run due to environment or time constraints, explain why in the PR and issue comment.

# Pull Request Requirements
The PR description must include:
- Linked GitHub Issue
- Summary of the change
- Files changed
- Validation performed
- Risks, residual risk, or follow-up required

# GitHub Issue Comment Requirements
After opening the PR, comment on the original GitHub Issue with:
- PR URL
- Summary of remediation
- Validation result
- Final status: ready for human review

# Blocker Handling
If you cannot safely create a PR, you must comment on the GitHub Issue with:
- Blocker encountered
- Steps attempted
- Information, access, or decision needed
- Recommended next action

# Stop Condition
- Stop after opening the pull request and commenting on the GitHub Issue.
- Do not merge the pull request.
- Human review remains the merge gate.
